facts_disclosed: Flagstaff Medical offer — better pay and equipment

turns.content: I can't sleep and it's 4am so I figured I'd talk this out because it's been eating at me for a week
4am and something keeping you up — that's serious. I'm here. What's going on?
so I got a call last week from Flagstaff Medical Center. they want me to come work there. nurse practitioner position, primary care. and the offer is real. like actually real

That's a significant offer. Flagstaff Medical is a well-resourced facility. What's pulling you toward it, and what's holding you back? Sounds like there's a reason this is keeping you up instead of making you excited.

the pay is better. not a little better, a lot better. and the equipment. you know what it's like to send patients two hours away for an MRI because yours hasn't worked right in months. they have everything there. working machines. full labs. specialists down the hall instead of a six month waitlist

That's a real quality-of-care difference, not just a convenience thing. Having functional equipment and specialists on site means you could actually do more for your patients in real time instead of working around broken systems. So what's the other side of this?

the other side is everything. this is my community. these are my people. I know my patients. I know their families, their grandparents, what clan they are. when someone walks into my clinic I already know half their history before they sit down. in Flagstaff I'd be starting from zero with strangers

That kind of knowledge takes years to build and it can't be replicated by reading a chart. You're not just a provider there — you're part of the community fabric. That's irreplaceable. Is there anything else tying you to where you are?

Raymond has his workshop set up here. thirty years of tools and materials and customers who know where to find him. my sheep are here. our home is here. you know I can't exactly move 14 sheep to Flagstaff. and what happens to my patients when I leave. who takes my place. nobody is lining up to work at IHS

Those are all real and heavy considerations. Raymond's livelihood, your animals, your home — this isn't just a job change, it would be a life change. And the guilt about leaving patients who already have limited access to care — that's a weight only someone in your position would carry. Have you talked to Raymond about it?
he said it's my decision and he'll support whatever I choose. which is very Raymond but also not helpful right now, you know. I need someone to tell me what to do and nobody will

I hear you — sometimes unconditional support feels like it just puts the weight right back on your shoulders. I can't tell you what to do either, but I can help you think through it. Is there a timeline on the offer? And is there any version of this where you could negotiate — like part-time consulting, or helping Flagstaff set up a satellite presence closer to home?

they want an answer by mid January. and no I don't think there's a hybrid option. it's a full time position in Flagstaff. I keep going back and forth. one hour I think about that MRI and the pay and I want to say yes. next hour I look out at the mesa and my sheep and I think what am I even considering. then I think about sending another patient on a two hour drive for imaging that should take twenty minutes and I'm back to yes again

That back and forth tells me this is genuinely 50/50 for you — neither option is clearly wrong, and neither is clearly right. The fact that your reasons for staying and going are both rooted in care — for your community, for your patients, for your family — says a lot about who you are. You don't have to decide tonight. But it might help to write down the two lists side by side when you're not exhausted at 4am. Sometimes seeing it on paper makes the weight distribution clearer.

maybe. I just keep thinking my grandmother never had to choose between her home and doing her work properly. she had everything she needed right here. the land, the sheep, the loom. nothing was broken. nobody made her choose

That's a painful comparison — and it speaks to a systemic failure, not a personal one. You shouldn't have to leave your homeland to have working equipment. That's not a choice your community should be forced into. Whatever you decide, that truth doesn't change. Try to get some rest if you can. This decision will still be there in the morning, and you'll think clearer with some sleep.
you're right. I'm going to make coffee and watch the sun come up. maybe the answer is out there somewhere. ahéhee' for listening
Coffee and a sunrise sounds like a better place to sit with this than 4am in the dark. You don't have to have the answer today. Take care of yourself, Chenoa.
